short.label: Short note
short.useCase: Use for narrow observations, quick reactions, or a single source-backed point.
expectations: Use one concrete example or source signal.
Avoid pretending the topic is fully settled.

standard.label: Standard essay
standard.useCase: Default for durable public essays that should show real judgment rather than daily filler.
expectations: Develop a clear thesis with enough room for nuance.
Include concrete failure modes, architectural boundaries, and at least one practical example.
Address the strongest obvious counterargument or tradeoff.
End with a practical consequence for builders, teams, or readers.

deep.label: Deep piece
deep.useCase: Use for pinned topics that need real argument, historical context, multiple examples, or a reference-heavy treatment.
expectations: Build the argument in layers instead of rushing to the takeaway.
Use multiple source signals or examples.
Separate claims, evidence, counterarguments, and design implications.
Prefer named boundaries and testable criteria over broad commentary.

styleRules: Write from inside real systems, not as a hype commentator.
Prefer concrete boundaries, failure modes, contracts, state, queues, tests, and human responsibility.
Do not sound like AI content. Keep the thesis human, specific, and accountable.
Avoid generic beginner explanations unless they are used to expose a real systems problem.
Use plain engineering language. Be direct, but do not posture.
Every draft should have a clear claim, a practical example, and a testable consequence.

styleExamples.title: Systems decide where risk lands
styleExamples.whyItWorks: It makes an abstract engineering claim concrete and moral without becoming theatrical.
styleExamples.text: Systems are not neutral; they decide who carries risk and who fights fires.

styleExamples.title: Harness over prompt
styleExamples.whyItWorks: It reframes AI quality as architecture, not prompt cleverness.
styleExamples.text: A useful AI workflow is less about the perfect prompt and more about the harness around it: inputs, contracts, review points, state, retries, and evidence.

styleExamples.title: Coherence over code volume
styleExamples.whyItWorks: It ties AI coding tools back to senior engineering judgment.
styleExamples.text: AI made the typing cheaper. It did not make coherence cheap.

lanes.label: Practical Agent Architecture
lanes.thesis: LLM systems become useful when they are treated as bounded software systems with contracts, state, evaluation, and recovery paths.
recurringAngles: why senior judgment matters more after AI

lanes.label: Procedural Fiction Systems
lanes.thesis: Procedural fiction needs state, consequence, memory, and authorial control before it becomes story instead of shuffled events.

lanes.label: AI Claims and Missing Receipts
lanes.thesis: AI product claims should be measured against harnesses, baselines, failure modes, and the actual work being replaced.

lanes.label: Rural Tech Reality
lanes.thesis: Technology that looks clean from the outside often has to survive messy local constraints, thin staffing, old systems, and real operational risk.
recurringAngles: systems that have to keep running

lanes.label: Self-Taught Engineering and Judgment
lanes.thesis: Credentials and competence overlap, but they are not the same thing; durable engineering judgment comes from responsibility, feedback, and hard systems.

pinnedTopics.title: An AI harness is not a prompt
pinnedTopics.thesis: A prompt can ask for behavior, but a harness decides what inputs are trusted, what outputs are valid, and what happens when the model is wrong.
openQuestions: What belongs in the harness instead of the prompt?
How much state should the model see?

pinnedTopics.title: Worldbuilding is a data architecture problem
pinnedTopics.thesis: A living fictional world needs relationships, pressure, memory, and constraints before a model can generate scenes that feel consequential.
openQuestions: How does author control stay separate from world simulation?

pinnedTopics.title: Where are the receipts?
pinnedTopics.thesis: When an AI company claims huge productivity or accuracy, the useful question is not whether AI can help. It is what baseline, workflow, and failure mode they measured against.
openQuestions: What would count as a fair baseline?
What work is being hidden in setup, review, and cleanup?
What happens when the model is confidently wrong?
